63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266

281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.

298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

302	
303	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md`. 
304

314	- Ref `references/hartshorne.md`: cited smooth criterion.
315	
316	## Notes / Strategy
317	- <ONLY if blocking or critical issue found. Keep to 1 sentence. Omit section if none.>

26	## Subagents

30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. 32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):

35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.

93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.

99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.

101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.

The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. 113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.

9	/-!
10	# Flat base change for the pushforward, global (`H⁰`-as-equalizer) chain
11	
12	This file builds the global ("FBC-B") leg of the `i = 0` flat-base-change package:
13	`H⁰(X, F) = Γ(X, F)` of a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme is the equalizer of
14	a *finite* affine cover, and flat base change commutes with that finite equalizer.
15	
16	It is the companion of `AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.FlatBaseChange`, which it imports
17	read-only (using the affine global-sections comparison as a per-term black box).
18	
19	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex` (FBC-B section).
20	-/

28	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — finite affine covers with quasi-compact overlaps -/
29	
30	/-- A quasi-compact scheme admits a *finite* affine open cover; when it is moreover
31	quasi-separated, every pairwise intersection of cover members is quasi-compact.
32	
33	Project-local: packages `isCompact_iff_finite_and_eq_biUnion_affineOpens` (finite affine
34	subcover of `⊤`) with `quasiSeparatedSpace_iff_forall_affineOpens` (quasi-compact overlaps)
35	into the single combinatorial input feeding the finite sheaf-condition equalizer of the
36	`H⁰` flat-base-change argument. -/
37	theorem Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact (X : Scheme.{u})
38	    [CompactSpace X] [QuasiSeparatedSpace X] :
39	    ∃ s : Set X.affineOpens, s.Finite ∧ (⨆ i ∈ s, (i : X.Opens)) = ⊤ ∧
40	      ∀ U ∈ s, ∀ V ∈ s, IsCompact ((U : Set X) ∩ (V : Set X)) := by
41	  obtain ⟨s, hs, he⟩ :=
42	    (isCompact_iff_finite_and_eq_biUnion_affineOpens (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mp
43	      (by simpa using isCompact_univ (X := ↥X))
44	  refine ⟨s, hs, he.symm, ?_⟩
45	  intro U _ V _
46	  exact quasiSeparatedSpace_iff_forall_affineOpens.mp ‹_› U V
47	
48	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — the global sections as a sheaf-condition equalizer -/
49	
50	open TopCat.Presheaf SheafConditionEqualizerProducts in
51	/-- For `M : X.Modules` and any open cover `U : ι → X.Opens`, the sheaf-condition fork
52	of the underlying abelian-group presheaf of `M` is a limit (an equalizer of products):
53	```
54	Γ(M, ⨆ i, U i) ⟶ ∏ i, Γ(M, U i) ⇉ ∏ (i,j), Γ(M, U i ⊓ U j).
55	```
56	This is the equalizer-products form of the sheaf condition specialised to the abelian
57	presheaf `M.presheaf` of a sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules. Combined with the finite affine cover
58	of `Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact` it computes `Γ(X, M) = Γ(M, ⊤)`
59	as a *finite* equalizer; that finiteness is what the flat-base-change argument needs to
60	commute `- ⊗_A B` past the equalizer.
61	
62	Project-local: packages `M.isSheaf` through Mathlib's
63	`isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts` at the level of `X.Modules`. -/
64	noncomputable def Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules)
65	    {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens) :
66	    IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U) :=
67	  ((isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts M.presheaf).mp M.isSheaf U).some
68	
69	open TopCat.Presheaf SheafConditionEqualizerProducts in
70	/-- Consolidation of the two preceding supplements: for a quasi-compact, quasi-separated
71	scheme `X` and `M : X.Modules`, there is a *finite* affine open cover `U : ι → X.Opens`
72	(`ι` finite, each `U i` affine, `⨆ i, U i = ⊤`, all pairwise intersections quasi-compact)
73	for which `Γ(X, M) = Γ(M, ⊤)` is the equalizer of the finite sheaf-condition fork of `U`.
74	
75	Project-local: combines `Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact` (L1) with
76	`Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork` (L2) into the directly-usable "global sections as a
77	finite equalizer" input of the flat-base-change argument. -/
78	theorem Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork (X : Scheme.{u})
79	    [CompactSpace X] [QuasiSeparatedSpace X] (M : X.Modules) :
80	    ∃ (ι : Type u) (_ : Finite ι) (U : ι → X.Opens),
81	      (∀ i, IsAffineOpen (U i)) ∧ (⨆ i, U i = ⊤) ∧
82	      (∀ i j, IsCompact ((U i : Set X) ∩ (U j : Set X))) ∧
83	      Nonempty (IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U)) := by
84	  obtain ⟨s, hs, hcov, hqc⟩ := X.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact
85	  have hfin : Finite ↥s := hs.to_subtype
86	  refine ⟨↥s, hfin, fun i => ((i : X.affineOpens) : X.Opens), fun i => i.1.2, ?_, ?_,
87	    ⟨Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork M _⟩⟩
88	  · rw [← hcov, iSup_subtype']
89	  · rintro ⟨i, hi⟩ ⟨j, hj⟩
90	    exact hqc i hi j hj

92	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — global sections as an `A`-module `eqLocus`
93	
94	This block presents `Γ(X, M)` of a sheaf of modules as a `LinearMap.eqLocus` of two
95	`A`-linear maps over the ground ring `A = Γ(X, ⊤)`, the shape required by
96	`LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv` (flat base change preserves finite equalizers). Every
97	section over an open `U` is viewed as an `A`-module by restriction of scalars along the
98	structure-sheaf restriction `A → Γ(X, U)`, and the structure-sheaf restriction maps of
99	`M` become `A`-linear maps between these. -/
100	
101	/-- The ground ring `A = Γ(X, ⊤)` of a scheme, as a `CommRing` (taken from the
102	`CommRingCat`-valued structure presheaf so the `CommRing`/`Algebra` instances resolve). -/
103	abbrev groundRing (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.presheaf.obj (Opposite.op (⊤ : X.Opens))
104	
105	/-- The structure-sheaf restriction ring hom `A = Γ(X, ⊤) → Γ(X, U)`. Project-local:
106	the `A`-algebra structure on the sections over `U` used to view them as `A`-modules. -/
107	noncomputable def rhoU (X : Scheme.{u}) (U : X.Opens) :
108	    groundRing X →+* (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.obj (Opposite.op U)) :=
109	  (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE (le_top)).op).hom
110	
111	/-- The sections `Γ(M, U)` of a sheaf of modules over an open `U`, regarded as an
112	`A`-module (`A = Γ(X, ⊤)`) by restriction of scalars along `rhoU`. Project-local: the
113	common `A`-module home for the global-sections equalizer presentation. -/
114	noncomputable abbrev gammaModA {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) (U : X.Opens) :
115	    ModuleCat (groundRing X) :=
116	  (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (rhoU X U)).obj (M.val.obj (Opposite.op U))
117	
118	/-- Restriction-of-scalars transitivity: `(Γ(X,U) → Γ(X,V)) ∘ (A → Γ(X,U)) = (A → Γ(X,V))`.
119	Project-local glue making the structure-sheaf restriction maps `A`-linear. -/
120	theorem rhoU_comp {X : Scheme.{u}} {U V : X.Opens} (h : V ≤ U) :
121	    ((X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE h).op).hom).comp (rhoU X U) = rhoU X V := by
122	  ext a
123	  change (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE h).op).hom (rhoU X U a) = rhoU X V a
124	  have e : (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE (le_top) : V ⟶ ⊤).op)
125	      = (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE (le_top) : U ⟶ ⊤).op)
126	          ≫ (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE h).op) := by
127	    rw [← X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map_comp]; rfl
128	  simp only [rhoU, e, RingCat.hom_comp]; rfl
129	
130	/-- The structure-sheaf restriction `Γ(M, U) → Γ(M, V)` (`V ≤ U`) as a morphism of
131	`A`-modules, built from `M.val.map` by restriction of scalars. -/
132	noncomputable def gammaResAHom {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {U V : X.Opens} (h : V ≤ U) :
133	    gammaModA M U ⟶ gammaModA M V :=
134	  (ModuleCat.restrictScalars (rhoU X U)).map (M.val.map (homOfLE h).op) ≫
135	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App (rhoU X U)
136	        (X.ringCatSheaf.obj.map (homOfLE h).op).hom (rhoU X V)
137	        (rhoU_comp h).symm (M.val.obj (Opposite.op V))).inv
138	
139	/-- The structure-sheaf restriction `Γ(M, U) → Γ(M, V)` (`V ≤ U`) as an `A`-linear map.
140	Project-local: the building block of the `leftRes`/`rightRes` legs of the equalizer. -/
141	noncomputable def gammaResA {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {U V : X.Opens} (h : V ≤ U) :
142	    gammaModA M U →ₗ[groundRing X] gammaModA M V := (gammaResAHom M h).hom
143	
144	@[simp] theorem gammaResA_apply {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {U V : X.Opens} (h : V ≤ U)
145	    (x : gammaModA M U) :
146	    gammaResA M h x = (M.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom x := by
147	  simp only [gammaResA, gammaResAHom, ModuleCat.hom_comp, LinearMap.comp_apply,
148	    ModuleCat.restrictScalars.map_apply, ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App_inv_apply]
149	
150	/-- Functoriality of the `A`-linear restriction maps. -/
151	theorem gammaResA_comp {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {U V W : X.Opens} (h1 : V ≤ U)
152	    (h2 : W ≤ V) (x : gammaModA M U) :
153	    gammaResA M h2 (gammaResA M h1 x) = gammaResA M (h2.trans h1) x := by
154	  simp only [gammaResA_apply]
155	  change (M.presheaf.map (homOfLE h2).op) ((M.presheaf.map (homOfLE h1).op) x)
156	     = (M.presheaf.map (homOfLE (h2.trans h1)).op) x
157	  rw [← CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.comp_apply, ← M.presheaf.map_comp]
158	  congr 1
159	
160	/-- The `leftRes` leg `∏ᵢ Γ(M,Uᵢ) → ∏ᵢⱼ Γ(M, Uᵢ ⊓ Uⱼ)` (restrict the `i`-th factor),
161	as an `A`-linear map. Project-local: the first leg of the sheaf-condition equalizer in the
162	`A`-module presentation. -/
163	noncomputable def leftRes {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens) :
164	    (∀ i, gammaModA M (U i)) →ₗ[groundRing X] (∀ p : ι × ι, gammaModA M (U p.1 ⊓ U p.2)) :=
165	  LinearMap.pi (fun p => (gammaResA M (inf_le_left)).comp (LinearMap.proj p.1))
166	
167	/-- The `rightRes` leg `∏ᵢ Γ(M,Uᵢ) → ∏ᵢⱼ Γ(M, Uᵢ ⊓ Uⱼ)` (restrict the `j`-th factor),
168	as an `A`-linear map. -/
169	noncomputable def rightRes {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens) :
170	    (∀ i, gammaModA M (U i)) →ₗ[groundRing X] (∀ p : ι × ι, gammaModA M (U p.1 ⊓ U p.2)) :=
171	  LinearMap.pi (fun p => (gammaResA M (inf_le_right)).comp (LinearMap.proj p.2))
172	
173	/-- The `A`-linear map `Γ(M, ⊤) → ∏ᵢ Γ(M, Uᵢ)` restricting a global section to the cover. -/
174	noncomputable def toCover {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens) :
175	    gammaModA M (⊤ : X.Opens) →ₗ[groundRing X] (∀ i, gammaModA M (U i)) :=
176	  LinearMap.pi (fun _ => gammaResA M le_top)
177	
178	/-- The restriction of a global section to a cover is a compatible family: it lands in the
179	`eqLocus` of the two restriction legs. Project-local: the equalizer-membership feeding the
180	global-sections `eqLocus` presentation. -/
181	theorem leftRes_toCover {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens)
182	    (s : gammaModA M (⊤ : X.Opens)) :
183	    leftRes M U (toCover M U s) = rightRes M U (toCover M U s) := by
184	  funext p
185	  simp only [leftRes, rightRes, toCover, LinearMap.pi_apply, LinearMap.comp_apply,
186	    LinearMap.proj_apply, gammaResA_comp]
187	
188	/-- The global-sections-to-compatible-families map corestricted to the `eqLocus`. -/
189	noncomputable def toCoverEqLocus {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u} (U : ι → X.Opens) :
190	    gammaModA M (⊤ : X.Opens) →ₗ[groundRing X] LinearMap.eqLocus (leftRes M U) (rightRes M U) :=
191	  (toCover M U).codRestrict _ (leftRes_toCover M U)
192	
193	/-- **Global sections as an `A`-module equalizer.** For `M : X.Modules` and an open cover
194	`U` of `X` (`⨆ i, U i = ⊤`), the global sections `Γ(X, M) = Γ(M, ⊤)`, regarded as a module
195	over the ground ring `A = Γ(X, ⊤)`, are `A`-linearly isomorphic to the `eqLocus` of the two
196	restriction legs `leftRes`, `rightRes : ∏ᵢ Γ(M, Uᵢ) → ∏ᵢⱼ Γ(M, Uᵢ ⊓ Uⱼ)`.
197	
198	Project-local: this is the `LinearMap.eqLocus` presentation of global sections over the
199	ground ring `A` that `LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv` (flat base change preserves finite
200	equalizers) consumes for the `H⁰` flat-base-change argument. Injectivity is the separatedness
201	of the sheaf of modules (`TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'`) and surjectivity is the gluing
202	axiom (`TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'`), both on the underlying `Ab`-sheaf `M.presheaf`. -/
203	noncomputable def gammaTopEquivEqLocus {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u}
204	    (U : ι → X.Opens) (hU : iSup U = ⊤) :
205	    gammaModA M (⊤ : X.Opens) ≃ₗ[groundRing X] LinearMap.eqLocus (leftRes M U) (rightRes M U) :=
206	  LinearEquiv.ofBijective (toCoverEqLocus M U)
207	    ⟨by
208	      intro s t hst
209	      have h := Subtype.ext_iff.mp hst
210	      refine TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq' (⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩ : TopCat.Sheaf Ab X)
211	        U ⊤ (fun _ => homOfLE le_top) hU.ge _ _ ?_
212	      intro i
213	      have hi := congrFun h i
214	      simpa only [toCoverEqLocus, LinearMap.codRestrict_apply, toCover, LinearMap.pi_apply,
215	        gammaResA_apply] using hi,
216	     by
217	      rintro ⟨sf, hsf⟩
218	      have hcompat : TopCat.Presheaf.IsCompatible M.presheaf U sf := by
219	        intro i j
220	        have h := congrFun hsf (i, j)
221	        simpa only [leftRes, rightRes, LinearMap.pi_apply, LinearMap.comp_apply,
222	          LinearMap.proj_apply, gammaResA_apply] using h
223	      obtain ⟨s, hs, -⟩ := TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'
224	        (⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩ : TopCat.Sheaf Ab X) U ⊤ (fun _ => homOfLE le_top) hU.ge sf hcompat
225	      refine ⟨s, ?_⟩
226	      apply Subtype.ext
227	      funext i
228	      simpa only [toCoverEqLocus, LinearMap.codRestrict_apply, toCover, LinearMap.pi_apply,
229	        gammaResA_apply] using hs i⟩
230	
231	/-- **Flat base change commutes with the `H⁰` equalizer.** For `M : X.Modules`, a cover `U`
232	of `X` (`⨆ i, U i = ⊤`), and a flat `A`-algebra `B` (`A = Γ(X, ⊤)`), base changing the global
233	sections `Γ(X, M)` along `A → B` is the `eqLocus` of the base-changed restriction legs:
234	\[ B ⊗_A Γ(X, M) ≅ \operatorname{eqLocus}(B ⊗ \mathrm{leftRes},\ B ⊗ \mathrm{rightRes}). \]
235	
236	Project-local: the composite of the equalizer presentation
237	`gammaTopEquivEqLocus` with `LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv` (flatness commutes with the finite
238	equalizer). This is the module-level core that the `H⁰` flat-base-change reduction consumes:
239	the right-hand side is, by the same presentation applied to the base-changed sheaf, the global
240	sections of the pulled-back module over `B`. -/
241	noncomputable def baseChangeGammaEquiv {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) {ι : Type u}
242	    (U : ι → X.Opens) (hU : iSup U = ⊤) (B : Type u) [CommRing B] [Algebra (groundRing X) B]
243	    [Module.Flat (groundRing X) B] :
244	    TensorProduct (groundRing X) B (gammaModA M (⊤ : X.Opens)) ≃ₗ[B]
245	      LinearMap.eqLocus (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.lTensor B B (leftRes M U))
246	        (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.lTensor B B (rightRes M U)) :=
247	  (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.congr (LinearEquiv.refl B B)
248	      (gammaTopEquivEqLocus M U hU)) ≪≫ₗ
249	    LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv B B (leftRes M U) (rightRes M U)
250	
251	end AlgebraicGeometry

3727	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3728	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3729	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3730	  \mathlibok
3731	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3732	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3733	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3734	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3735	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3736	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3737	  \[
3738	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3739	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3740	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3741	  \]
3742	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3743	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3744	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3745	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3746	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3747	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3748	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3749	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3750	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3751	\end{lemma}

3776	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3777	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3778	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3779	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3780	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3781	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3782	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3783	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3784	  \begin{proof}
3785	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3786	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3787	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3788	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3789	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3790	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3791	  \end{proof}
3792	\end{lemma}

4218	The separated-case argument is repackaged here at the level of \(A\)-modules, where
4219	\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf, so that flat
4220	base change becomes the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
4221	(Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) applied to an honest
4222	\(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\). The blocks below construct that presentation concretely: every
4223	module of sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\) is viewed as an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along the
4224	structure-sheaf restriction \(A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), the restriction maps of \(M\) become
4225	\(A\)-linear, and the two product legs of the sheaf condition assemble into a pair of \(A\)-linear maps
4226	whose equalizer locus is \(\Gamma(X, M)\). Throughout, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is a sheaf of
4227	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) is an open cover with
4228	\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).

4231	abelian-group sheaf \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), recorded as Mathlib dependency anchors.

\section{FBC-B: the global \texorpdfstring{$H^0$}{H0} flat-base-change isomorphism via the finite affine-cover equalizer (direct route)}

The blocks of the preceding subsection assemble into a \emph{direct} proof of the named global
isomorphism that bypasses the adjoint-mate keystone of the affine/mate route. The route is
\emph{{\v C}ech-cohomology-free}: \(H^0\) is a finite limit — the equalizer of a finite affine cover —
and the flat functor \(-\otimes_A B\) preserves finite limits, so the global comparison isomorphism
follows from per-chart affine base change \emph{without} the abstract conjugate/mate identification and
without the Mayer--Vietoris induction.

Concretely, the module-level core
\(B \otimes_A \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \cong \operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{leftRes},
\mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{rightRes})\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_baseChangeGammaEquiv}) already commutes flat
base change past the finite \(H^0\) equalizer of a cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\). The single remaining step
is to recognise the right-hand base-changed equalizer, chart by chart, as the equalizer-locus
presentation (Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus}) of the global sections of the pulled-back module
over the base-changed cover \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) of \(X' = X_B\). The theorem below states that assembly.

\begin{theorem}[Global \(H^0\) flat base change via the finite-cover equalizer, direct route]
  \label{thm:fbcb_global_direct}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.baseChangeGammaPullbackEquiv}
  \uses{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs, lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus,
        lem:fbcb_baseChangeGammaEquiv, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
        lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), part (2), $i = 0$ case
  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L966--968).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), part (2), \(i = 0\) case.}
  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme with ground ring
  \(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and
  let \(B\) be a flat \(A\)-algebra. Write \(X' = X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the
  pullback. Then the comparison map
  \[
    \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; \Gamma(X', \mathcal{F}')
  \]
  is a \(B\)-linear isomorphism, assembled directly from the finite affine-cover equalizer presentation;
  consequently the sheaf-level base-change map \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F}) \to f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is an
  isomorphism, recovering the named global target Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}. The
  route uses neither the adjoint-mate keystone of the conjugate route nor the Mayer--Vietoris induction
  on the number of cover members.

  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\item We have $\psi^* \widetilde M = \widetilde{S \otimes_R M}$
  % functorially in the $R$-module $M$." (references/stacks-schemes.tex, Tag 01I9, part (1) —
  % the per-chart pullback-is-base-change identification feeding the assembly.)
  \begin{proof}
    \uses{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs, lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus,
          lem:fbcb_baseChangeGammaEquiv, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
          lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}
    Choose a finite affine cover \(\{U_i\}_{i\in\iota}\) of \(X\) with quasi-compact pairwise overlaps
    (Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}), so that \(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).

    \emph{Flat base change past the finite equalizer.} By
    Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_baseChangeGammaEquiv}, flatness of \(B\) over \(A\) gives a \(B\)-linear
    isomorphism
    \[
      B \otimes_A \Gamma(X,\mathcal{F}) \;\cong\;
        \operatorname{eqLocus}\bigl(\mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{leftRes},\ \mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{rightRes}\bigr),
    \]
    where \(\mathrm{leftRes}, \mathrm{rightRes} : \prod_i \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
    \prod_{i,j}\Gamma(U_i\cap U_j,\mathcal{F})\) are the two restriction legs of the cover.

    \emph{Per-chart identification of the base-changed legs.} The base-changed cover
    \(\{(U_i)_B\}_{i\in\iota}\) is again a finite affine cover, now of \(X'\): base change preserves
    affineness and open immersions and carries \(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\) to \(\bigvee_i (U_i)_B = \top\).
    On each chart the affine pullback dictionary (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, Stacks
    Tag 01I9: \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^*\widetilde M \cong \widetilde{B\otimes_A M}\)) reads the
    sections of \(\mathcal{F}'\) over \((U_i)_B\) as the base change of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\),
    \[
      \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}'\bigr) \;\cong\; \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
    \]
    and likewise on each overlap, where the affine base-change lemma
    (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) supplies
    \(\Gamma((U_i\cap U_j)_B, \mathcal{F}') \cong \Gamma(U_i\cap U_j,\mathcal{F})\otimes_A B\). These
    identifications are natural in restriction, so they intertwine the base-changed legs
    \(\mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{leftRes}\), \(\mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{rightRes}\) with the
    restriction legs \(\mathrm{leftRes}'\), \(\mathrm{rightRes}'\) of the cover \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) of
    \(X'\); hence they restrict to an isomorphism of equalizer loci
    \[
      \operatorname{eqLocus}\bigl(\mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{leftRes},\ \mathrm{id}_B\otimes\mathrm{rightRes}\bigr)
      \;\cong\;
      \operatorname{eqLocus}\bigl(\mathrm{leftRes}',\ \mathrm{rightRes}'\bigr).
    \]

    \emph{Recognising the right-hand equalizer.} Applying the equalizer-locus presentation
    (Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_gammaTopEquivEqLocus}) to \(\mathcal{F}'\) and the cover \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) of
    \(X'\) identifies the latter equalizer locus with the global sections,
    \(\operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes}',\mathrm{rightRes}') \cong \Gamma(X',\mathcal{F}')\).
    Composing the three isomorphisms yields the \(B\)-linear isomorphism
    \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X',\mathcal{F}')\), which is the comparison map.

    \emph{Back to the sheaf-level statement.} Finally, the reduction
    Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections} — each pushforward is the tilde of its module
    of global sections, and pullback along \(g\) is \(-\otimes_A B\) on modules — turns this module
    isomorphism into the isomorphism of the sheaf-level base-change map
    \(g^*(f_*\mathcal{F}) \to f'_*\mathcal{F}'\), the named global target
    Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}. No conjugate/mate identification and no {\v C}ech
    cohomology beyond the degree \(0/1\) sheaf condition enters.
  \end{proof}
\end{theorem}
